speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three with Morris Kozak. Today is August the 9th, 1996. Morris, you want to tell us which-- what was happening? You were hiding in another farm now.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were there, then we start to hear the cannons, the-- from something. The earth start to tremble from the cannons. They were fighting. But we didn't know where it was coming. Who knows how far they are. We were there, I'll say, about two weeks. The Russians came. We were liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Early spring in-- this was in 1944 already. I don't remember the dates. We lived like-- like-- like a dog don't know the date. We didn't know no dates. Most we knew was either Monday or was Tuesday. That's all-- that's all we knew. And we were liberated by the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we pointed fingers wherever we had to point, that Ukrainian, that Savitskii, the one who was looking for us so hard. There's some other ones there. They were collaborators with the Germans, big collaborators. And the Russians, whatever they had to do, had-- they had-- they did. And we stayed there, well, there was one--

speaker: interviewer
text: You stayed where you continued staying in the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple day, few days. And one officer, I think he was-- he was a Jewish officer. And we start to talk to him. He says, look, he says. This is a war. We're here today. The war, we can be 50 kilometers further. But we could be 30 kilometers back. And if the Germans ever come here and find you, you're dead. Sure enough, we went out on a highway. We were free. And trucks were running through the city of Rovno. It was 50 kilometers away from us.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell Rovno?

speaker: interviewee
text: R-O-V-N-O, Rovno. And sure enough, they give us a lift. And they bring us. And we seen a lot of--

speaker: interviewer
text: This is with your whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whole family.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we see him dead, a lot of dead soldiers, equipment, you know, laying. Anyway, we went all the way to Rovno. Come to Rovno, we find a place, a house, an empty place. And we got in there. We were there for about a week or so, and they start to mobilize young people. I was already 18 or 19 years old in those days. All of a sudden, I heard that they're taking people with trades. In other words, they're given them-- if they have a trade, they valuable. They shouldn't be sent on the front. I used to be a-- I used to, my younger kid, when I was a kid, I used to hang out at the blacksmith. I was a hanger there. I used to help him with this, help him with that. I knew a little bit about being a-- I signed myself up as a blacksmith. I went in there. Me and my brother, we sign ourselves up, and we became-- now, we got the [NON-ENGLISH]. That [NON-ENGLISH] means they cannot touch us to take us-- take us to the front. So they took us away to Kiev from Rovno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Took us to Kiev, and they put us on a working battalion. I signed myself up as a blacksmith. They made a-- a-- a-- what do you call it-- a-- [POLISH] what do you call it? When a salesmen just send in-- in the windows. What do you-- what do you call that man? A glazier?

speaker: interviewee
text: A glazier. They made a glazier out of me from a blacksmith. I learned a little bit here and there. Anyway, we worked there. I worked--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your bother with you in--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was there with me too. He was-- was different kind of work. And wants someone to send-- to send me away. Khrushchev had a big-- they called a [NON-ENGLISH], a resort place-- on the River Dnieper. There was a-- he had a big [NON-ENGLISH] there, and everything was destroyed. So they send away maybe 300 or 400 people there. And I was the glazier. I was the-- the main guy there to setting the-- the windows. And I was there living in the village. And they didn't have much themselves. So whatever, we used to steal something. Everything was-- I need a lot of little glass in their houses here. I used to sell it for a little bit of milk, a piece of bread. This was a way of life. One time I got almost caught, and they would have send me away to Siberia. Anyway from there-- then I came back to Kiev after the-- after the summer. In Russia, if you didn't steal, you couldn't exist during the war. Whatever you-- you handled, it was an item. So everything was an item. They brought us back to-- to Kiev. And the war went on all the way to Poland. The war was finished already. It was in 1945. The war was ended. I was there quite a while. All of a sudden I hear they want to send us further deep into Russia. We wanted to go back this way, back to Poland. We didn't want to go back to Poland. We want to go out. Anyway, with-- I had a-- a secretary there. She helped me with-- call it [NON-ENGLISH]. She made me out two-- two passes, like. You couldn't buy a ticket on the railroad unless you have that-- that pass. Anyway, me and my brother-- the only thing she didn't fill in is the date. I filled in the dates. And we went away. We bought two. We went back to-- my father and mother were living in a-- it's not far from-- Mlynow was no more Jews there-- in a big old town called Dubna. They lived there, my father and mother. This--

speaker: interviewer
text: Dubna was how far from [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: 20 Kilometers. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that your parents were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we-- we knew. After the war, we knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were in Rovno together. They then went from Rovno. They went to Dubna. We used to write letters to one another. Anyway, we bought tickets. And we-- we were supposed to go-- the tickets fro Kiev to Lvov. But it-- this-- the train stopped in Dubna. So we jumped off the train. And my father already signed up that we were Polish citizens. We were Polish until 1939. But we wanted to get out the Soviet Union. So he signed everybody up that we are Polish citizens. We want to go back to our-- to our motherland, to Poland. Sure enough, a lot of people are the Polacks. They were shipping out all the Polacks from there and a lot of Jews. They sign up. They want to remain there. Came to Poland. We came to a town named-- to Selesia. They took us to Selesia in Poland, that time a town called Bytom. And we got settled there. I was there about three, four weeks with my-- my parents, my-- everybody. And all of a sudden, we heard on the bazaar, they killed a few Jews in the Polish bazaar, in-- in-- in Bytom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who killed the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some Polacks, anti-Semites. We decided-- I-- I came home. I say, and the Zionist movement was working already in Poland. Couldn't work in Russia, but in Poland was a Zionist movement working. So we got connected with them. I came home one day. I say to Mom and Pop, I'm leaving. Where you going? I said I'm going to Palestine at that time. We got together 10 boys and one girl. I'll never forget. And the Zionist movement already moved us, gave us papers. We are refugees from here, refugees. They knew what to do. So we went from Poland to Czechoslovakia. From--

speaker: interviewer
text: When they say we, who is the we?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 10 boys--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left my family in Bytom. And we came to Czechoslovakia. From Czechoslovakia, we got over to Austria, to Vienna. Got over to Vienna, and it was-- the crowd was getting bigger and bigger, hundreds and hundreds of people. And we crossed to Vienna from the Soviet zone through the-- probably through an American zone. Then we had to walk a long way. It-- that's a lot, you know, by the time we got there. And we were like in a camp. Then they took us all, and they shipped us to Germany. The Zionist movement shipped us all to Germany not far from Munich to a DP camp. No. While I'm in-- I'm running away with myself. While I'm in Austria, and I signed up to go to Italy, to go to Palestine illegal. There were a lot of people there. And I was supposed to leave in two, three days. While I'm an Austria, somebody says, Kozak, your family came. They're looking for you. Sure enough, my parents came. My brother came. And my two sisters came to Austria. After they left, maybe a week later or two weeks later, and they went on the same-- same route. Well, they came. Then I went into the office. I say, look, my whole family just came. I think I'm going to hold off right now to go to Italy. And we'll see what-- what has happened. Anyway, we were there for a few weeks or so. And they shipped us to Germany, the whole family. We were united again. Shipped us to Germany, and we-- they-- they took us into a DP camp. We became displaced people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who was it that shipped you? Was it the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the-- the-- the Americans already. The American shipped us from Austria with the-- with the guidance of the-- of the Zionist movement. So we got in a-- a camp called Ferenwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-E-R-E-N-W-A-L-D, probably, Ferenwald. And we want to camp [INAUDIBLE]. We were there maybe 5,000 Jews. Became displaced person, they give us a place to live. And that was it. Then later,

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what was it like? How-- how long were you there in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The [NON-ENGLISH] gave us food.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a while. I can't recall the times.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came here probably about a year. This was in '46 already. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like in the-- in the camp for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The camp was-- wasn't easy. But the [NON-ENGLISH] used to give us food. You had kitchens, you know? And whatever we could do a little bit on-- on my own, you know, buy this, sell this, you know, to survive. Now, later we find they opened up the-- a quota to come here to this country, I think. And we registered. And-- and somehow, my father had a brother. He came here in the first World War. And my father put in the name. And he find us here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father-- What was your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jake Kassek. We-- he changed his name when he came over, probably. He used to write his name Kassek.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-A-S-S-E-K or C-K, whatever. From Kozek they change it to either to Kassek. And anyway, we got in contact with him. I don't know if he helped us from here. We came through the IS. We registered to go to America. We went through all kind of examinations here and there. Finally, our papers came-- came through. And we went to Bremen, Bremerhaven in-- in Germany. And we stayed there for about a week or two. We came here on a boat called It was a Liberty boat. And we came here in 1947, I believe, on 1 March. And it opened up a whole new world. Didn't know the language. Didn't have no money. Didn't have nothing. Didn't know a soul. Went out and got a job.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you came here, where-- where did you come? Where did you stay, and what city where you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got a place. We couldn't get an apartment. We got a place 2nd and Pine.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, here, Philadelphia, 2nd and Pine. Over-- there were a lot of wholesale fruit-- fruit distributors there. Downstairs was distributing fruit. And we had an apartment upstairs. And we lived there for a while. I got a job. My father got a job. My brother was already married. He got married in Germany. He came here with his wife, and a kid, and a baby. He had a different place somewhere, 4th and [? Ritna ?] somewhere. He got a place. He knew welding. He got a job as a welder. I got a job as a meat cutter in a wholesale place. And I worked in that job for 13 years here in this country. Met my wife. We got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- what's your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Loretta.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Borden, B-O-R-D-E-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49, December-- December the 17th of '49. And I worked very hard. I worked for about 13 years as a meat cutter in a wholesale place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you happen to get a job as a meat cutter? Did you know anything about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew-- I was a farm boy. I knew a little bit of everything. I knew a little bit of everything. And I-- I picked up the trade very fast. And I worked there for 13 years, and I became top man there. But I wasn't happy working for somebody else.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after 13 years, my father-- my brother was a welder. Then he took a gas station. And that-- my brother says to him-- me-- why don't you try a gas station? I said, I don't know nothing about a gas station. I don't know how to check oil in a car. Anyway, he got in touch after-- I worked there for 13 years, and it was getting rougher and rougher and rougher. He got in touch with the-- the salesman from the Esso company. Met me here and there. I-- He says, there's a station coming up in Mount Airy we're building at Houston Road and [? Chelton ?] Avenue. He says, would you be interested in it? I said, sure. Why not? I had two debts. I went-- they send me to a school, to a training school on City Line Avenue. Took a course. I took the station. And I figured out one time, the hours I'm putting in, I'm working for $0.50 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I left the job, I was making $3.00 an hour. But here, I'm working for myself for $0.50 an hour. But I was determined. I'll make a go of it. Sure enough, I made a go of it. And it-- it was a successful station. I stood there for about almost 12 and 1/2, 13 years. Now, me and my brother, he had a gas station too on Frankfurt, at Frankfurt Avenue. We decided we're going to build our own place. So we build a place here on Bustleton Avenue, 9600. It called Kozak's Tire Center. And we were very successful. After eight years, the family got bigger. His son got bigger, and my son's got bigger. It wasn't enough for everybody. Anyway, we decided one of us has to go. So they bought me out. Him and his son bought me out. And from that business, I-- I bought out another business in-- in Kensington, which I didn't know nothing about it. And-- and I took in my two sons with me. I was there a few years with them. And they running it now. And thank God, we're doing all right. And this is-- this is the story of my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Morris, could you tell me. You mention the sons that you have. How many children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The older one is Jeff, Alan, and David. And thank God, they're fine-- they're fine kids. We have seven grandchildren. And me and my wife, we lead a nice-- nice life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Want to tell me the names of your--

speaker: interviewee
text: Worked very hard. The names of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The names of your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest one is Evin. Then we have Andrea. Then we have Alex. Then we have Justin. Then we have James, and Tommy, and Jared.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. During the war, a lot of people had to make some very hard choices. What-- what was the hardest choice that you had to make during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, I don't think there was very little choices. You lived by your instinct. You lived by your-- I believe in fate, not being smart, no such a thing. Wasn't smart, nobody was smart of us. Fate it the way you led you to it, instead you should be here, you should be there. Because here, see what happened? Here, you were saved. But you didn't know that. I mean, there's no such a thing as-- I-- I believe more in fate than anything else. And it was no choice, was no-- the way it led you, that you're supposed to go. That's my belief.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now that it's been more than 50 years since the end of the war has passed, do you have a particular message or something you would like to say that you would like to-- your grandchildren or your children to remember or the world at large that you like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The world has a lot of wonderful people. There's a lot of bad people. And-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] and there's a big distinction. You always have to look after the good people, not to the bad people. The world is meant for everybody. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And everybody has a right to live. That's my message.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Morris, tell me what this represents.

speaker: interviewee
text: This represents a-- a-- this is a memorial book which was created in Israel from the town Mlynov and an adjacent town, Muravica. And this is written different articles for different people, survivors, people before the war, from the town Mlynov and Muravica. And I have a whole book of it. I have-- and this is the cover from the books, from the book.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is a family picture taken before the war. From the right to the left is my mother. We had a younger brother named Kalman. He died at the age of 11. And this is my-- in the middle is my older brother named Rubin. Next to Rubin is my father, which is Isak. And I'm in front of my father, which is Morris, and my sister, Jean, on the bottom, and my youngest sister, Karen, on top. This picture, my uncle had it here in the United States. We didn't have any pictures from there, from Europe, from before the war. But my uncle had it. And when we came here, we made quite a few prints. And this is one of the pictures. That's all we have from Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, approximately, I imagine was taken around 1935, '36, in that era.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know where this would have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Mlynov.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in 1948 after we arrived in the States. The picture is my younger sister on the right hand side, sister Karen, then me, Morris, and-- and then my father is holding his first grandson, my father Icak, and his name is [? Mort. ?] In back of my father is my sister, Jean. Next to my father is my mother-- my mother, Chava. In back of my mother is my brother, Rubin and his wife, Sylvia. The-- this was taken a year after we came to the United States. This picture is our wedding picture when we got married in 1949, December the 17th. This is me and my wife, Loretta. Her maiden name was Loretta Borden. This is a wedding picture of my younger son, David, and Jan, which he got married October, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: You want to-- what was the date on that again? Was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: October--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll clarify that a little later.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. From right to left is my father-in-law, Herman Borden, and back of him, my son Jeff. Next to my Jeff is his wife [? Laurie. ?] Next to my father-in-law is my mother-in-law, [? Rae, ?] [? Rae ?] Borden. Next to my daughter-in-law is-- is me, Morris, and the bride, Jan, and my son, David. And in the front is my wife, Loretta, my mother Chava. And next to my mother is my father, Icak. Next to David is my daughter-in-law, Harriet, and my other son, Alan. This was the wedding picture of my son, David. On a previous picture, I made a little mistake. The picture was taken in 1981. This picture was taken May, 1990. This picture is-- I brought over the granddaughter of Anopro. Her name is Vera Kukhar. I brought her over here to this country in 1990. And she spent with us four weeks. This picture is my father, Icak, my mother, Chava, and Vera Kukhar. They're sitting and talking, which was-- which was a very, very pleasurable thing to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is this woman, who--

speaker: interviewee
text: This woman is the granddaughter, the one who used to bring us the food through the pigsty. She was 11 years old in those days. And I brought over here for a visit. This picture was taken May, '94. And my sister Jean is on it, and my sister Karen, myself, Morris, my father, Icak, and my brother, Rubin. This was taken in May, and my father passed away in 1990. November 19, 1994, my father passed away. Morris, you want to tell us who on the right is which, from going from right to left, who they are? My sister Jean Litz, my other sister, Karen Lowenthal, myself, Morris Kozak, and Rubin Kozak, and Icak Kozak, my father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is all my grandchildren, my wife, and myself. This picture was taken in November, 1995, at a Hanukkah party at our house. From the right is Justin Kozak. Next to him is Alex Kozak, myself. Next to them is Evin Kozak, Jared Kozak. Next to them is Andrea Kozak, my wife Loretta, my grandson James. In the back of him is his brother, Tommy Kozak.
